LLM wiki actually works so you
understand what's happening. There are
three layers. The first is your raw
sources, documents, articles, papers,
data files. These are immutable. The AI
reads from them but never changes them.
Your source of truth. The second is the
wiki itself. A folder of AI generated
markdown files, summaries, entity pages,
concept pages, comparisons, a synthesis.
The AI owns this layer. It creates
pages, updates them when new sources
arrive, maintains cross references, and
keeps everything consistent. You read
it, the AI writes it. The third is the
schema, a configuration document that
tells the AI how the wiki is structured,
what the conventions are, and what to do
when you add new sources or ask
questions. This is what makes the AI a
disciplined knowledge organizer rather
than just a generic chatbot. You and the
agent evolve this together over time.
There are three core operations. Ingest,
you drop a new source in and tell the AI
to process it. It reads the document,
pulls out key takeaways, writes a
summary page, updates the index, and
touches any relevant pages across the
wiki. A single source might update 10 to
15 different pages. That's where the
compounding starts. Query, you ask
questions. The AI searches relevant

with citations. Here's a smart insight
